content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Amber Maltbie 
Attorney at Law 
Nossaman LLP 
777 S. Figueroa Street, 34th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90017 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-17-278 
Dear Ms. Maltbie: 
March 14, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Huntington Beach City 
Councilmember Billy O'Connell regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request 
as one for infonnal assistance.2 You may contact us for formal advice when specific decisions and 
facts are available for analysis. 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also, the Fair Political Practices Commission {"Commission") does not act 
as a finder of fact when rendering advice, and this letter is based on the facts presented. (In re 
Oglesby (1975) l FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember O'Connell, with various interests including residential rental 
properties within 500 feet of the Beach and Edinger Corridors Specific Plan ("BECSP"), take part 
in decisions amending the development standards for the entire BECSP, changing the overall 
BECSP Maximum Amount of Net New Development ("MAND") for residential development, or 
relating to the MAND lawsuit?3 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the rcquestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. {Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c){3),) 
3 You have also requested advice on additional hypothetical decisions affecting property more than 500 feet 
from the ccouncilmember's rental properties. However. each of these decisions require a case-by-case analysis based 
upon specific facts. We are unable to provide assistance regarding these decisions without an adequate description of 
the nature of the decision. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. 1-17-278 
Page No. 2 
In light of the councilmember's interest in residential rental properties within 500 feet of 
the BESCP, Councilmember O'Connell may not participate in these decisions based upon the facts 
provided. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR4 
Councilmember O'Connell has served on the Huntington Beach City Council since 
November 2014. The Beach and Edinger Corridors Specific Plan ("BECSP") was adopted by the 
Huntington Beach City Council in March 2010, with a purpose to orchestrate private and public 
investment activities along two primary commercial corridors: Beach Boulevard and Edinger 
Avenue. Overall, the BECSP comprises 459 acres along these two commercial corridors. For 
planning purposes, the two corridors are divided into multiple geographic areas or 
"segments" each with its own set of development standards. 
Since the approval of the BECSP in 2010, community consensus has changed and the 
BECSP was amended in April 2015. The primary effect of the amendments was to reduce the 
Maximum Amount of Net New Development ("MAND") of residential units from 4,500 units to 
2, I 00, of which approximately 1,900 had already been entitled and/or built. This reduction in 
residential capacity prompted a lawsuit by a regional non-profit organization, which advocates for 
the creation of affordable housing opportunities. The lawsuit is unresolved at this time. 
Councilmember O'Connell recused himself from the BECSP amendment vote held in April 
2015 due to the proximity of his real property and business interests located in Huntington Beach. 
However, additional City Council considerations will take place in the future. These votes are 
expected to include decisions amending the development standards for the entire BECSP, changes 
to the MAND for residential development, and deliberations relating to the MAND lawsuit. 
Councilmember O'Connell owns three residential properties from which he receives rental 
income that are located within 500 feet of the BECSP boundary. Councilmember O'Connell also 
receives income in his capacity as Executive Director of Collette's Children's Home, an Internal 
Revenue Code 50l(c)(3) charitable nonprofit. Collette's Children's Home owns two residential 
properties within 500 feet of the BECSP boundary. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the public official's interests as set forth in Section 87103, which includes: 
4 These facts have been revised pursuant to your email dated February 26, 2018. 


File No. 1-17-278 
Page No. 3 
• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of$2,000 or 
more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution 
made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, 
received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision 
is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
Relevant to the BECSP and MAND decisions, Councilmember O'Connell has a business 
entity interest in his rental property business and real property interests in the three residential rental 
properties located within 500 feet of the BECSP boundary. He also has interests in Colette's 
Children's Home, and in the tenants of his three rental properties, as sources of income. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 1870l(a) provides that the effect of a decision is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the official's interest is explicitly involved in the decision. As detailed in Regulation 
18701(a), an official's interest is explicitly involved in a decision if it is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision.5 From the limited information provided, it appears that the official's 
interest will not be explicitly involved in decisions amending the development standards for the 
entire BECSP, changing the MAND, or relating to the MAND lawsuit. 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides that if a financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect 
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable; however, if the financial result cannot 
be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not 
reasonably foreseeable. 
Property Interest: Residential Rental Property 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) through (12) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a 
decision's financial effect on an official's interest in real property that is not explicitly involved in 
the decision. In pertinent part, a financial effect is material if the decision: 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property . . .. 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice 
5 An interest is the "subject" of the decision if it involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation 
of any license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Real property is explicitly involved as the 
"subject" of a decision ifil is affected as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l) through (6). 


File No. I-17-278 
Page No. 4 
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission 
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably 
foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property;" 
Business Interest: Rental Property Business 
Regulation 18702. l (b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial 
effect on an official's interest in a business entity that is not explicitly involved in the decision. 
Under Regulation 18702.1 (b ), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the 
value of the business entity. Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides the following types of decisions that 
may have a material effect on a business entity: 
"(l) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business entity is engaged; 
"(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the 
business entity is engaged; 
"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the 
business entity supplies; 
"(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may 
affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity 
receives; 
"(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or 
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws 
from the location of the project; or 
"(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of 
the business entity." 
The recent amendments to the MAND cut the planned residential units in the BECSP area in 
half. The reduction prompted a lawsuit by a regional non-profit organization, which advocates for 
the creation of affordable housing opportunities. Councilmember O'Connell is in the business of 
renting residential properties, and has three properties located within 500 feet of the BECSP 
boundaries. 
As stated above, Councilmember O'Connell may not take part in the decisions unless the 
Commission finds sufficient facts to indicate there is no foreseeable measurable impact on the 
properties. Based upon the information provided, we find there are insufficient facts to indicate 
there are "no reasonably foreseeable measurable impacts" on his real property from decisions 
amending the development standards for the entire BECSP, changing the MAND, or relating to the 
MAND lawsuit under Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 1). Moreover, changes to the MAND will affect the 


File No. 1-17-278 
Page No. 5 
residential rental market and are also likely to implicate factors (1 ), {2), and (3) of Regulation 
18702.1 (b ), above. Therefore, Councilmember O'Connell may not take part in these decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant Chief Counsel 
tf 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

3 You have also requested advice on additional hypothetical decisions affecting property more than 500 feet

the BESCP, Councilmember O'Connell may not participate in these decisions based upon the facts 
provided.

more (Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

(Section 87103(b).)

made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to,

allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission
